title: Jazz
intro: Jazz questions in quizbowl trace the genre's stylistic evolution roughly chronologically. Early jazz and swing supply bandleaders like Duke Ellington, Louis Armstrong, and Count Basie, whose orchestras defined the music's first commercial peak. Bebop follows with Charlie Parker, Dizzy Gillespie, Thelonious Monk, and Bud Powell, who traded swing's danceable riffs for fast, harmonically dense improvisation centered on clubs like Birdland. Miles Davis anchors the next stretch of the canon almost single-handedly, appearing across cool jazz (Kind of Blue) and fusion (Bitches Brew) alike, while John Coltrane, Sonny Rollins, and McCoy Tyner represent the hard bop and post-bop saxophone tradition that followed bebop.

A smaller but consistent cluster covers jazz's departures from the mainstream lineage: Ornette Coleman and Charles Mingus for free jazz and genre-blending composition, and Herbie Hancock and Chick Corea for the electric fusion era. Jazz vocalists — Ella Fitzgerald, Nina Simone, and the blues tradition behind Bessie Smith — form their own recurring cluster, often tested through signature songs like "Strange Fruit" rather than through instrumental technique.

Instruments and general jazz vocabulary make up a large share of the distribution: piano, saxophone, trumpet, trombone, guitar, drums, and double bass are tested both as answerlines in their own right and as connective tissue between performers. Countries and regions outside the United States — Brazil and bossa nova, Cuba and Afro-Cuban rhythm, France and Gypsy jazz, Nigeria and Afrobeat — round out the canon, reflecting jazz's absorption of, and export to, musical traditions worldwide.

sections.name: Early Jazz and Swing
sections.blurb: New Orleans jazz through the big-band swing era and its bandleaders.

entries.note: Bandleader-composer who led his orchestra for decades at the Cotton Club; wrote "Take the A Train" and "Mood Indigo."
New Orleans trumpeter and gravelly-voiced vocalist who popularized scat singing; led the Hot Five and Hot Seven.
Riff-based swing standard associated with Count Basie's orchestra.
Swing-era clarinetist and bandleader famous for "Begin the Beguine."
Cornetist of the 1920s known for a lyrical, melodic improvisational style distinct from hot jazz's aggression.
Bandleader and showman known for the scat-filled "Minnie the Moocher" and its "hi-de-ho" call and response.
Kansas City bandleader-pianist known for a spare, riff-driven big-band swing style; led the orchestra behind "One O'Clock Jump."
Stride pianist, organist, and comic vocalist known for "Ain't Misbehavin'" and "Honeysuckle Rose."

sections.name: Bebop
sections.blurb: The fast, harmonically complex 1940s rebellion against swing-era convention.

entries.note: Trumpeter and bebop co-founder known for his bent-bell trumpet and puffed cheeks; frequent partner of Charlie Parker.
Alto saxophonist and bebop pioneer nicknamed "Bird," known for blistering improvisation; namesake of the club Birdland.
Pianist-composer known for angular melodies and dissonant harmony; wrote "'Round Midnight" and "Straight, No Chaser."
Fast, harmonically complex jazz style of the 1940s pioneered by Parker, Gillespie, and Monk, emphasizing virtuosic improvisation.
Legendary New York City jazz club named after Charlie "Bird" Parker, a hub of the bebop and hard bop scenes.
Pianist who translated bebop's fast horn lines to the keyboard, influencing generations of jazz pianists.
Pianist-composer known for angular melodies and dissonant harmony; wrote "'Round Midnight" and "Straight, No Chaser."

sections.name: Cool Jazz and Modal Jazz
sections.blurb: Miles Davis's relaxed 1950s cool sound and its move toward modal, scale-based improvisation.

entries.note: Lyrical pianist known for impressionistic harmony; played on Kind of Blue and led his own trio with Scott LaFaro.
Pianist known for unusual time signatures, notably the 5/4 "Take Five" from the album Time Out.
Trumpeter who shaped multiple jazz eras from cool jazz to modal jazz to fusion; led Kind of Blue and Bitches Brew.
Miles Davis's best-selling modal jazz album featuring Bill Evans and John Coltrane, including "So What."
Dave Brubeck Quartet's album exploring unusual time signatures, including the 5/4 hit "Take Five."

sections.name: Hard Bop and Post-Bop
sections.blurb: Bebop's bluesier, gospel-inflected successor and the saxophone-led quartets that followed it.

entries.note: Tenor and soprano saxophonist known for "sheets of sound" improvisation; recorded the spiritual suite A Love Supreme.
Sonny Rollins's acclaimed album featuring the calypso-inflected tune "St. Thomas."
John Coltrane's four-part spiritual jazz suite expressing gratitude and devotion.
Trumpeter known for a bright, virtuosic tone on hard bop and post-bop recordings.
Pianist known for modal, quartal-harmony playing as a member of John Coltrane's classic quartet.
Tenor saxophonist known for extended unaccompanied solos and the calypso-tinged "St. Thomas."

sections.name: Free Jazz and the Avant-Garde
sections.blurb: Mid-century abandonment of fixed chord changes and conventional structure.

entries.note: Virtuoso bassist-composer known for a volatile temperament and genre-blending works like Mingus Ah Um.
Alto saxophonist who pioneered free jazz's abandonment of fixed chord changes; recorded The Shape of Jazz to Come.

sections.name: Jazz Fusion
sections.blurb: Jazz's merger with rock, funk, and electric instrumentation from the late 1960s onward.

entries.note: Pianist who moved from Miles Davis's quintet to electric funk-jazz fusion; composed "Watermelon Man" and the album Head Hunters.
Miles Davis's 1970 double album blending jazz improvisation with rock rhythms and electric instruments, a founding fusion text.
Genre blending jazz improvisation with rock, funk, and electric instrumentation, pioneered by Miles Davis and Herbie Hancock.
Pianist who played with Miles Davis before founding the fusion band Return to Forever.

sections.name: Jazz Vocalists
sections.blurb: Singers who defined jazz's vocal tradition, from scat to blues-inflected protest song.

entries.note: "The First Lady of Song," celebrated for pure tone, wide range, and scat singing virtuosity.
Pianist-vocalist blending jazz, blues, and classical influences with civil-rights-themed songs like "Mississippi Goddam."
Wordless vocal improvisation using nonsense syllables, popularized by Louis Armstrong and Ella Fitzgerald.
Haunting anti-lynching protest song made famous by Billie Holiday's stark vocal performance.
Likely refers to Bessie Smith, the "Empress of the Blues," a foundational blues vocalist whose recordings shaped early jazz singing.

sections.name: Latin, Caribbean, and Global Jazz
sections.blurb: Rhythms, countries, and scenes outside the United States that shaped jazz's development.

entries.note: Brazilian fusion of samba rhythm and cool jazz harmony, popularized by Joao Gilberto and Antonio Carlos Jobim.
Source of Afro-Cuban rhythms fused into jazz by Dizzy Gillespie and conga player Chano Pozo.
Home of bossa nova and samba, whose rhythms fused with jazz through Jobim and Joao Gilberto.
Home to the West Coast cool jazz scene of the 1950s, centered around Los Angeles studio musicians.
Trinidadian rhythm and song style that influenced jazz tunes like Sonny Rollins's "St. Thomas."
Armenian double-reed woodwind occasionally incorporated into world-jazz fusion recordings.
Spanish guitar and dance tradition that influenced jazz fusion, notably Miles Davis's Sketches of Spain.
Home to Gypsy jazz guitarist Django Reinhardt and violinist Stephane Grappelli's Quintette du Hot Club de France.
Home to the ECM record label, known for atmospheric European jazz recordings.
Home to a devoted jazz audience and record-collector culture, plus pianist Toshiko Akiyoshi.
Home to Afrobeat, Fela Kuti's genre fusing jazz, funk, and West African rhythms.
Home to pianist Abdullah Ibrahim and trumpeter Hugh Masekela's township jazz tradition.

sections.name: Instruments, Standards, and Motifs
sections.blurb: The instruments, tunes, and general vocabulary that recur across jazz's subgenres.

entries.note: Central jazz instrument, showcased by soloists from Art Tatum and Bud Powell to Bill Evans and Herbie Hancock.
Reed instrument central to jazz improvisation, epitomized by Charlie Parker's alto and John Coltrane's tenor and soprano playing.
Brass instrument with a sliding tube, prominent in swing-era big bands and Dixieland ensembles.
String instrument used in jazz from Django Reinhardt's Gypsy jazz to Wes Montgomery's octave technique.
Brass instrument central to jazz solos, from Louis Armstrong and Dizzy Gillespie to Miles Davis.
Reed instrument central to early New Orleans jazz and swing-era clarinetists like Benny Goodman.
Percussion kit driving jazz rhythm, played by innovators like Max Roach, Art Blakey, and Elvin Jones.
Guitar style within jazz spanning Django Reinhardt's Gypsy swing to Wes Montgomery's octave-based soloing.
Upright string bass providing jazz's walking-bass foundation, played by Charles Mingus and Ray Brown.
Jazz standard famous for Coleman Hawkins's landmark 1939 tenor saxophone improvisation.
Refers to the double bass or electric bass providing jazz's harmonic and rhythmic foundation.
Twelve-bar chord progression and vocal tradition foundational to jazz improvisation and repertoire.
Canadian virtuoso pianist known for dazzling technique and swinging trio recordings.
Mallet percussion instrument prominent in jazz through players like Lionel Hampton and Milt Jackson.
Hard bop standard composed by Bobby Timmons, popularized by Art Blakey's Jazz Messengers.
Likely refers to Nat "King" Cole, pianist-turned-vocalist known for smooth ballads like "Unforgettable."
American composer who bridged jazz and classical music with Rhapsody in Blue and the opera Porgy and Bess.
Spontaneous, in-the-moment composition central to jazz performance across all its subgenres.
String instrument less common in jazz than others, notably played by Stephane Grappelli and Stuff Smith.

appendix.answer: The Shape of Jazz to Come
